title: Tanker report - Week 1 2016
summary:
VLCCs
The reduction in the new 2016 Worldscale flat rates has led to spot rates increasing as owners look for equivalent 2015 returns. Thus we are now seeing rates for long east of around WS 107.5/110 on 270,000 tonnes with rates to the US Gulf hovering in the very low WS 60s. Brokers are saying rates remain under pressure here as charterers work quietly and for those who can take either new-buildings, ex-drydock or tonnage over 15 years old a discount is achievable.

children.title: VLCCs
children.summary: The reduction in the new 2016 Worldscale flat rates has led to spot rates increasing as owners look for equivalent 2015 returns. Thus we are now seeing rates for long east of around WS 107.5/110 on 270,000 tonnes with rates to the US Gulf hovering in the very low WS 60s.

children.title: Suezmaxes
children.summary: It has been an active week in West Africa and levels have edged up from WS 110 to WS 115. In the Black Sea, Turkish Straits delays of around five to six days each way mean that Black Sea rates have continued to firm and after Chevron took Seacross for 135,000 tonnes at WS 132.5, Transway subsequently took a Shell re-let basis 140,000 tonnes at WS 140.

children.title: Aframaxes
children.summary: Rates in the Mediterranean drifted down from WS 130 at the start of the week to settle at WS 115 level. Poor weather now is creating delays and uncertain itineraries for tonnage and brokers feel that levels will remain steady as charterers focus on either prompt vessels or vessels that have definite berthing prospects.

children.title: Panamaxes
children.summary: Off the Continent, in view of the weak market in the Caribbean, there has been a steady supply of ballasters, keeping rates at around WS 145 for 55,000 tonnes from ARA back to US Gulf.

children.title: Clean
children.summary: A volatile week in the market for 37,000 tonnes from Continent to USAC led to an initial jump in rates of 20 points to a high of WS 160 before reduced levels of enquiry saw the market ease to WS 150, with ice class tonnage seeking a premium for going transatlantic. In the 38,000 tonnes backhaul trade from the US Gulf, rates dropped throughout the week from WS 130 to as low as WS 105 before recovering modestly to WS 110 as sheer volume of fixing for both short and longer haul trips helped significantly thin the tonnage list.
